# Appendix B: Quarto Cross-Reference Guide {#sec-appendix-b .unnumbered}

This appendix provides a complete reference for Quarto's cross-referencing system, covering figures, tables, equations, sections, and theorems.

## How Cross-References Work {#sec-crossref-overview .unnumbered}

Quarto's cross-reference system has two parts:

1. A **label** on the item being referenced (must start with `fig-`, `tbl-`, `eq-`, `sec-`, etc.)
2. A **reference** in text using `@label`

Quarto then numbers items automatically and inserts the number wherever `@label` appears.

## Figures {#sec-crossref-figures .unnumbered}

### ggplot2 / Base R Figures {#sec-fig-code .unnumbered}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: fig-yield-trend
#| fig-cap: "Annual wheat yield trend in Punjab (2010–2023)."
#| fig-alt: "Line chart showing increasing wheat yield over time."
#| fig-width: 8
#| fig-height: 4.5

ggplot(wheat_data, aes(x = year, y = yield)) +
  geom_line() + ...
```
````

Reference in text: `As shown in @fig-yield-trend, yields have increased...`

**Rendered:** "As shown in Figure 1, yields have increased..."

### Multiple Figures (Sub-figures) {#sec-subfigs .unnumbered}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: fig-comparison
#| fig-cap: "Yield comparison across crops."
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Wheat yields by state"
#|   - "Rice yields by state"
#| layout-ncol: 2

plot_wheat
plot_rice
```
````

Reference sub-figures: `@fig-comparison-1` and `@fig-comparison-2`.

### Static Images {#sec-fig-static .unnumbered}

```markdown
![Caption for the image.](images/my-figure.png){#fig-my-image width=80%}
```

Reference: `@fig-my-image`.

---

## Tables {#sec-crossref-tables .unnumbered}

### knitr Tables {#sec-tbl-knitr .unnumbered}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-summary-stats
#| tbl-cap: "Summary statistics for the wheat yield dataset."

knitr::kable(summary_table)
```
````

Reference: `@tbl-summary-stats`.

### gt Tables {#sec-tbl-gt .unnumbered}

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-regression-results
#| tbl-cap: "Regression coefficients for the yield model."

library(gt)
my_table |> gt()
```
````

### Markdown Tables {#sec-tbl-markdown .unnumbered}

```markdown
| State  | Yield (qt/ha) | Area (000 ha) |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Punjab | 50.2          | 3520          |

: Summary of wheat production {#tbl-wheat-states}
```

Reference: `@tbl-wheat-states`.

---

## Equations {#sec-crossref-equations .unnumbered}

### Display Equations {#sec-eq-display .unnumbered}

```markdown
The linear model is:

$$
Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_i + \varepsilon_i
$$ {#eq-linear-model}

where $\varepsilon_i \sim N(0, \sigma^2)$.
```

Reference: `As shown in @eq-linear-model...`

### Inline Math {#sec-eq-inline .unnumbered}

Inline equations use single dollar signs: `$\hat{\beta} = (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty$`

### Common LaTeX Math Symbols {#sec-latex-symbols .unnumbered}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-latex
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Common LaTeX math symbols used in statistics"

symbols <- data.frame(
  Symbol = c("$\\mu$", "$\\sigma$", "$\\sigma^2$", "$\\bar{x}$",
             "$\\hat{\\beta}$", "$\\sum$", "$\\prod$", "$\\int$",
             "$\\alpha$", "$\\beta$", "$\\epsilon$", "$\\varepsilon$",
             "$\\sim$", "$\\approx$", "$\\neq$", "$\\leq$",
             "$H_0$", "$H_1$", "$\\chi^2$", "$\\infty$"),
  Meaning = c("Population mean", "Population SD", "Population variance",
              "Sample mean", "Estimated coefficient", "Summation",
              "Product", "Integral", "Significance level / Type I error",
              "Regression coefficient", "Error term", "Residual",
              "Distributed as", "Approximately equal", "Not equal",
              "Less than or equal", "Null hypothesis", "Alternative hypothesis",
              "Chi-squared", "Infinity"),
  LaTeX = c("\\mu", "\\sigma", "\\sigma^2", "\\bar{x}",
            "\\hat{\\beta}", "\\sum", "\\prod", "\\int",
            "\\alpha", "\\beta", "\\epsilon", "\\varepsilon",
            "\\sim", "\\approx", "\\neq", "\\leq",
            "H_0", "H_1", "\\chi^2", "\\infty")
)

knitr::kable(symbols, col.names = c("Symbol", "Meaning", "LaTeX Code"))
```

---

## Sections {#sec-crossref-sections .unnumbered}

Section cross-references use the section's label (the `{#sec-...}` anchor):

```markdown
## Methods {#sec-methods}

... content ...

As described in @sec-methods, we used...
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Quarto Auto-Labels

Quarto automatically creates labels for headings based on their text. "## My Analysis" gets the label `#sec-my-analysis`. You only need to specify `{#sec-...}` explicitly if you want a custom label.
:::

---

## Theorems and Proofs {#sec-theorems .unnumbered}

```markdown
::: {#thm-clt}
## Central Limit Theorem

Let $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ be i.i.d. random variables with mean $\mu$ and 
variance $\sigma^2$. Then as $n \to \infty$:

$$
\frac{\bar{X}_n - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0, 1)
$$
:::

Reference: @thm-clt states that...
```

Other theorem-type blocks: `{#def-...}` (Definition), `{#lem-...}` (Lemma), `{#cor-...}` (Corollary), `{#prp-...}` (Proposition), `{#exm-...}` (Example).

---

## Citations {#sec-citations-ref .unnumbered}

### Basic Citations {#sec-basic-cite .unnumbered}

```markdown
<!-- Inline citation -->
@wickham2016r found that...

<!-- Parenthetical citation -->
The Grammar of Graphics [@wilkinson2005grammar] describes...

<!-- Multiple citations -->
Several studies [@ref1; @ref2; @ref3] have shown...

<!-- Suppress author name -->
The package [-@wickham2016r] provides...

<!-- Page numbers -->
@wickham2016r [p. 42] states...
```

### `.bib` File Format {#sec-bib-format .unnumbered}

```bibtex
@book{wickham2016r,
  title     = {R for Data Science},
  author    = {Wickham, Hadley and Grolemund, Garrett},
  year      = {2016},
  publisher = {O'Reilly Media},
  url       = {https://r4ds.had.co.nz}
}

@article{wilkinson2005grammar,
  title   = {The Grammar of Graphics},
  author  = {Wilkinson, Leland},
  year    = {2005},
  journal = {Springer},
  doi     = {10.1007/0-387-28695-0}
}
```

---

## Cross-Reference Prefixes Summary {#sec-prefix-summary .unnumbered}

```{r}
#| label: tbl-prefixes
#| echo: false
#| tbl-cap: "Quarto cross-reference label prefixes and their usage."

prefixes <- data.frame(
  Type = c("Figure", "Table", "Equation", "Section", "Listing (code)",
           "Theorem", "Lemma", "Corollary", "Proposition",
           "Definition", "Example", "Exercise"),
  Prefix = c("fig-", "tbl-", "eq-", "sec-", "lst-",
             "thm-", "lem-", "cor-", "prp-",
             "def-", "exm-", "exr-"),
  Example_Label = c("#fig-scatter", "#tbl-summary", "#eq-linear",
                    "#sec-methods", "#lst-clean-code",
                    "#thm-clt", "#lem-helper", "#cor-result", "#prp-main",
                    "#def-pvalue", "#exm-ttest", "#exr-exercise1"),
  Reference = c("@fig-scatter", "@tbl-summary", "@eq-linear",
                "@sec-methods", "@lst-clean-code",
                "@thm-clt", "@lem-helper", "@cor-result", "@prp-main",
                "@def-pvalue", "@exm-ttest", "@exr-exercise1")
)

knitr::kable(prefixes, col.names = c("Type", "Prefix", "Example Label", "Reference Syntax"))
```
